title: The Problem: Sampling Isn't Differentiable
concept: Randomly sampling from a distribution is not a smooth, differentiable operation, which breaks backpropagation through the sampling step.

exercises.prompt: When you flip a coin and it lands on heads, can you predict exactly which tiny nudge caused 'heads' instead of 'tails'?

options.text: No — it just randomly landed that way, no smooth cause-effect link
Yes — flipping harder always gives heads
Yes — the coin's color decides it

exercises.explain: Exactly! Random outcomes don't have a smooth, traceable link back to a tiny adjustment. That's the core issue with sampling and gradients.

cards.title: The Magician's Hat Problem
cards.body: A magician pulls a random rabbit out of a hat. Cool trick — but if you asked 'which knob do I turn to get a bigger rabbit next time?', there's no knob! Random sampling in AI has the same mystery: it produces a result, but hides HOW that result connects to the settings that created it.

cards.title: What Does 'Not Differentiable' Mean?
cards.body: Differentiable means we can measure how a tiny change in the input causes a tiny, predictable change in the output — this is how neural networks learn. Random sampling just picks a value by chance, with no smooth link back to the settings (like the mean or spread) that produced it. So gradients can't flow backward through it.

cards.title: Like a Slot Machine Lever
cards.body: Imagine a slot machine. You pull the lever, and it randomly lands on a result. You can't ask 'how would the result change if I pulled the lever a tiny bit softer?' — the randomness breaks that smooth connection. A neural network's 'gradient' is like asking that question, and random sampling gives no answer.

cards.title: Guessing Tomorrow's Temperature
cards.body: Say a model predicts tomorrow's temperature will be around 20°C, with some randomness added for uncertainty. It samples and gets 23°C. If we ask 'how should I adjust my prediction to get a better sample?', we can't trace that back smoothly — the random dice roll hides the connection between the 20°C setting and the 23°C result.

cards.title: Myth: Random Just Means Messy Math
cards.body: Myth: 'Sampling is just noisy math, so gradients just get a little noisy too.' Truth: It's not about noise — it's that the sampling STEP itself has no derivative at all. There's no slope to compute, because a random draw is a jump, not a smooth curve. This is a structural problem, not a precision problem.

cards.title: Smooth Path vs. Random Jump
cards.body: Backpropagation needs a smooth, connected path from output back to input, like a ramp. Random sampling is more like teleporting — there's no ramp to walk back down.

left.text: Smooth curve — tiny input changes create tiny, trackable output changes. Gradients flow easily.

right.text: A sudden random jump to one outcome. No smooth curve exists, so gradients get stuck.

exercises.prompt: Why can't backpropagation flow through a random sampling step?

options.text: Because computers can't generate random numbers fast enough
Because random sampling has no smooth, differentiable connection between settings and the outcome
Because random numbers are always too large for neural networks
Because sampling only works with images, not numbers

exercises.explain: Right! The random draw is a sudden jump, not a smooth function, so there's no gradient (slope) to send backward through it.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Random sampling is just a 'noisy' differentiable function, so gradients still flow through it fine, just less accurately.
exercises.explain: False! It's not a matter of accuracy — sampling has NO derivative at all. It's a structural break in the smooth path gradients need.

exercises.sentence: Random sampling breaks backpropagation because it isn't ___, meaning tiny changes in settings don't create smooth, trackable changes in the output.
exercises.explain: 'Differentiable' means we can compute a smooth slope connecting inputs to outputs. Sampling lacks this smooth connection entirely.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best explains WHY gradients can't pass through a random sampling step?

options.text: Sampling produces a single random jump with no smooth slope connecting it back to the input settings
Sampling always produces numbers that are too big for computers to handle
Gradients can pass through fine, but only in image-based models

exercises.explain: Correct! There's no 'slope' to measure — a random draw jumps straight to a result, with no smooth in-between path for gradients to follow.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'Since sampling is random, we should just skip computing gradients for it and hope the rest of the network learns anyway.' What's the better mindset for solving this problem?

options.text: Accept it can never be solved and avoid randomness entirely in AI models
Recognize the sampling step blocks gradient flow, and look for a clever trick to make it smooth or bypass it (a problem to solve, not ignore)
Just make the random numbers smaller so gradients pass through easier
Ignore the issue since neural networks don't actually need gradients to learn

exercises.explain: Exactly right mindset! Sampling truly blocks gradients — but instead of giving up, researchers look for clever workarounds (like reparameterization tricks) to restore that smooth path. That's the problem this whole unit will solve!
